g.data-narration: QR Factorization decomposes a matrix A into Q times R, where Q is orthogonal with orthonormal columns and R is upper triangular.
QR Factorization expresses any rectangular matrix A as Q times R, where Q has orthonormal columns and R is upper triangular.
g.text: Q: Orthogonal matrix (Q^T Q = I), columns are orthonormal

g.data-narration: The Classical Gram-Schmidt process builds Q by orthogonalizing each column against all previous columns, then computing norms for proper normalization.
g.text: Given vectors a₁, a₂,..., compute orthonormal q's:

g.data-narration: The Modified Gram-Schmidt method provides better numerical stability by orthogonalizing each vector sequentially against all previously computed orthonormal vectors efficiently.
g.text: For each k, orthogonalize remaining columns against qₖ:

g.data-narration: Householder reflections transform a vector by reflecting it about a plane, using formula H equals I minus 2uu^T, to efficiently zero out elements below the diagonal.
g.text: Reflects vector x about a hyperplane to zero elements:

g.data-narration: QR factorization solves least squares problems by first computing Q transpose b, then solving the triangular system R x equals Q transpose b through back substitution.
g.text: For Ax ≈ b (m>n), solve using QR decomposition:

g.data-narration: Key computational insights include Q preserving Euclidean norms which ensures numerical stability, with Householder transformations optimal for dense matrices and Givens rotations suited for sparse matrices.
g.text: • Q preserves norms: ||Qx|| = ||x|| ensures numerical stability
• Householder: best for dense matrices | Givens: best for sparse matrices
